dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 187 for an unconscious adult at 1920 Turner St.
Hello, I'm Kevin, an EMT with the rescue squad. This is my partner, Maya. Can you tell us what happened?
Our patient is a 55-year-old male I found on the floor unconscious, but breathing. I'm his roommate.
Can you gently shake his shoulders and ask, "Hey, are you with us?" to assess his responsiveness?
No eye opening when I shook him. His eyelids don't move unless you physically open them.
Try shouting to see if he has a verbal response.
He briefly opened his eyes when I shouted. It's more responsive than earlier, but still minimal.
I'm applying a gentle pain stimulus. Watch for any limb movement.
Airway is patent. No secretions or obstructions. Patient is maintaining it on his own.
Breathing is shallow and irregular, at a rate of 22. I'm attaching the ETCO2 monitor now.
Radial pulse is present but thready. I'll take his blood pressure and SpO2 next.
Blood pressure is 103/50, SpO2 is 95% on ambient air. No cyanosis observed.
When did you arrive, and was there any prior behavior or signs beforehand?
I arrived 10 minutes ago and found him like this. No prior signs known to me.
Does he have a known medical history, like substance use or diabetes?
He has a history of opioid abuse. I don't know if he's diabetic.
Does he appear to be in pain or discomfort in any way?
No, he is completely unresponsive. I can't detect any sign of pain.
Skin is pink, cool, and dry with normal turgor. No rashes, bites, or bruising observed.
Head normal, no trauma. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive at 4 mm each.
No jugular venous distension or tracheal deviation observed.
I'll auscultate the lungs and listen to the heart rhythm. Rate matches with EKG findings.
Chest movement is symmetric with no trauma. Breathing sounds clear bilaterally.
Initial pulse of 129 at the radial artery.
Respiration rate is 22 per minute. Pattern is irregular and shallow, as noted.
Blood pressure is 103/50 to start-you can record that for baseline.
Glucose is elevated at 369 mg/dL. That's a sign of a potential hyperglycemic issue.
O2 saturation is at 95%. I've placed a nasal cannula at 2 liters per minute.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia with a rate of 129 bpm. No acute cardiac changes seen.
Altered mental status with high glucose and no immediate response - following protocol 3-15.
Confirming initiation of protocol 3-15 for altered mental status and possible overdose.
I've started a 20-gauge IV in the left AC. We are set for administering meds.
Administer 0.5 mg naloxone IV. Monitor for any improvement in consciousness now.
Given 0.5 mg naloxone. No change in mental status or breathing noted for now.
Pushing 100 mL of Normal Saline-this should help with perfusion or dehydration issues.
Current pulse is 113. Respiratory rate has dropped to 10. BP is 97/53 with an SpO2 of 95%.
Now at 104/70 BP and SpO2 up to 100% on 2L O2. Respiratory rate is 16 and more regular.
Naloxone didn't change condition. Fluids done. No other meds available per protocol.
No signs of pain. Still unresponsive, without awareness.
Transporting to Chippenham for an ED. It's the closest with excellent resources.
Patient is safely secured on the stretcher. No complications during lift.

Chippenham, this is Med 187. We have a 55-year-old male with altered mental status and a history of opioid abuse. Given 0.5 mg naloxone IV and 100 mL NS. Vitals include HR 113, BP 104/70, RR 16, SpO2 100% on 2L O2. Glasgow Coma Scale 11.
